data.title: Top Founder Led Stocks One Cash Earner One Dividend Engine One Underpriced
data.description:
With inflation paths, energy prices and interest rates all pulling markets in different directions, it helps to focus on one clear question: who is truly aligned with you as a shareholder? Founder led companies often stand out because the people making the big decisions typically have significant capital invested alongside you and a long-term legacy at stake. Beauty Tech Group (LSE:TBTG)

Overview: Beauty Tech Group provides a range of at home beauty devices and related skincare, selling LED masks, radio frequency tools, microcurrent devices and laser hair removal products under the CurrentBody Skin, ZIIP Beauty and Tria Laser brands through its own e commerce sites, marketplaces and retail partners around the world.

Operations: The group generates most of its £140.96m revenue from the CurrentBody segment (£125.78m), with smaller contributions from ZIIP (£13.16m), Tria (£1.95m) and third party sales (£0.08m), and meaningful exposure to the USA, UK, Europe and Asia.

Market Cap: £363.21m

Beauty Tech Group may appeal to investors who prefer founder led businesses that are already profitable and focused on at home consumer products. Revenue of £140.96m and net income of £9.93m indicate a business that is generating cash, while earnings have moved higher and net margins have widened to 7%, even as one off items and an £8.3m non recurring loss add complexity to the recent numbers. Analysts currently anticipate earnings growth and see a gap between their price targets and the current share price. However, the P/E of 36.6x and a capital structure fully reliant on external borrowings highlight potential risks if conditions tighten. These headline figures do not fully show how the balance of growth, pricing and funding risk compares for long term holders.

Beauty Tech Group’s widening 7% net margin and £9.93m profit sit against a punchy 36.6x P/E that could be masking key trade offs for long term holders, so review the 4 key rewards and 1 important warning sign

Computacenter (LSE:CCC)

Overview: Computacenter is an IT services company that helps large corporates and public sector clients plan, buy, implement and run their technology, covering everything from workplace devices and support to cloud, networking and security solutions across the UK, Europe and North America.

Operations: Computacenter generates about £9.19b of revenue almost entirely from Computer Services, with major exposure to the United States, Germany and the United Kingdom.

Market Cap: £4.30b

Computacenter sits at the heart of its clients’ everyday IT, handling procurement, support and managed services at scale, which can create sticky customer relationships and recurring revenue. The stock is priced below some cash flow based fair value estimates and has a dividend track record that may interest income focused investors, but investors also need to weigh a higher reliance on external borrowing and recent insider selling when deciding how comfortable they are with the risk profile.

Computacenter’s mix of sticky IT contracts, a share price sitting below some cash flow based fair value estimates, and insider selling raises questions about what the market might be missing, so review the 2 key rewards and 2 important warning signs
